incoming_email.subject: How do I re-waterproof my rain jacket?
incoming_email.body: I bought the Cascade Rain Shell jacket (order #NW-59934) about a year ago and I've noticed water isn't beading up on the surface like it used to — it seems to be soaking into the fabric instead. Is there something I should be doing to maintain the waterproofing, and is it too late to fix at this point?

sent_reply:
Hi Sofia,

Great question, and no, it's definitely not too late — what you're describing is completely normal wear on the durable water repellent (DWR) coating of the Cascade Rain Shell after about a year of use, and it's easy to restore.

First, wash the jacket on a gentle cycle with a technical gear cleaner (avoid regular detergent, which can leave residue that blocks water repellency). Then apply a spray-on or wash-in DWR treatment made for waterproof-breathable fabrics, and tumble dry on low heat or use a warm iron on a low, no-steam setting over a cloth — the heat reactivates the coating.

Doing this once or twice a season should keep your jacket beading water like new. Let me know if you'd like a specific product recommendation for the DWR treatment.

— Northwind Gear Support

incoming_email.subject: How do I figure out my backpack size before ordering?
incoming_email.body: I'm about to order my first real backpacking pack from you and I have no idea how backpack sizing works beyond shirt sizes. How do I know if I need small, medium, or large, and does it matter how tall I am versus my torso length specifically?

sent_reply:
Hi Ethan,

Congrats on your first real backpacking pack — happy to walk you through sizing, and you're right that it's torso length, not overall height, that matters most.

To measure: tilt your head forward and find the bony bump at the base of your neck (that's your C7 vertebra) — that's the top measurement point. Then find the imaginary line between the top of your hip bones (iliac crest) — that's the bottom point. Measure the distance between those two points along your spine; that's your torso length.

As a general guide: under 16 inches is typically Small, 16-19 inches is Medium, and over 19 inches is Large, though it varies slightly by model, so check the specific product page for exact ranges. If you tell me your torso measurement, I'm glad to double check which size fits best for the pack you're considering.

— Northwind Gear Support

incoming_email.subject: Best way to clean and store my tent after a trip?
incoming_email.body: Just got back from a muddy, rainy trip with the Alpine Vista 2-Person Tent (order #NW-72156) and want to make sure I store it properly so it doesn't get moldy or damaged before my next trip in a couple months. What's the recommended way to clean and dry it before packing it away?

sent_reply:
Hi Miguel,

Glad you made it back, and great instinct asking before packing away a muddy, wet tent — that's exactly how mold and mildew problems start.

First, set up the Alpine Vista fully (poles and all) somewhere dry and let it air dry completely, inside and out — do not pack it away even slightly damp. Once dry, brush off any dried mud or debris with a soft brush or damp cloth; avoid harsh detergents, which can strip the waterproof coating. If it needs a deeper clean, use a bit of mild, non-detergent soap and lukewarm water on the affected areas only, then rinse and dry fully again.

For storage, keep it loosely stuffed or rolled (not tightly compressed) in a breathable storage sack rather than its original stuff sack, in a cool, dry place out of direct sunlight. That will keep the fabric and waterproof coating in good shape until your next trip.

— Northwind Gear Support
